name: Chemical Kinetics
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Rate of a Reaction
sections.body:
Rate of reaction is the change in concentration of reactant or product per unit time. For aA + bB → cC + dD:

Rate = −(1/a) d[A]/dt = −(1/b) d[B]/dt = (1/c) d[C]/dt = (1/d) d[D]/dt.

Average rate is over a finite interval; instantaneous rate is the limit as Δt → 0 (slope of concentration–time curve).

Units: mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ (concentration/time). For gases, sometimes atm s⁻¹.

Factors affecting rate: nature of reactants, concentration, temperature, catalyst, surface area, light/radiation.

sections.heading: Rate Law and Order of Reaction
sections.body:
The rate law (or rate equation) is an experimentally determined relation: rate = k·[A]x·[B]y, where k is the rate constant and x, y are orders with respect to A and B (need not equal stoichiometric coefficients).

Overall order = x + y. Can be 0, 1, 2, 3, or fractional.

Molecularity: the number of molecules taking part in an elementary step. Always a positive integer (1, 2, rarely 3). Order = molecularity only for elementary reactions; for multi-step reactions, order is determined by the rate-determining (slowest) step.

Units of k depend on overall order n: k has units mol1−n Ln−1 s⁻¹.

  • Zero order: mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
  • First order: s⁻¹
  • Second order: L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹

sections.heading: Integrated Rate Equations
sections.body:
Zero-order reaction (rate = k): [R] = [R]0 − kt. Plot of [R] vs t is linear with slope −k. Half-life t1/2 = [R]0 / 2k (depends on initial conc). Examples: photochemical decomposition of HI on Au surface; decomposition of N2O on Pt.

First-order reaction (rate = k[R]): ln([R]0/[R]) = kt; equivalently k = (2.303/t) log([R]0/[R]). Plot of ln[R] vs t (or log[R] vs t) is linear with slope −k (or −k/2.303). Half-life t1/2 = 0.693/k (independent of initial concentration). Examples: radioactive decay, decomposition of N2O5, hydrolysis of ester in dilute acid (pseudo-first-order).

Pseudo-first-order: a true higher-order reaction that becomes first-order because one reactant (often solvent) is in large excess and its concentration is effectively constant. Example: hydrolysis of ester: CH3COOC2H5 + H2O → CH3COOH + C2H5OH; with H2O in excess, rate = k'·[ester].

sections.heading: Temperature Dependence — Arrhenius Equation
sections.body:
Rate constant rises sharply with temperature: roughly, k doubles for every 10 K rise. Quantitatively, the Arrhenius equation:

k = A · e−Ea/RT, where A is the pre-exponential / frequency factor, Ea the activation energy, R = 8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.

Taking log: ln k = ln A − Ea/RT or log k = log A − Ea/(2.303·RT). Plot of log k vs 1/T is a straight line with slope −Ea/(2.303·R), useful for finding Ea graphically.

Two-temperature form: log(k2/k1) = (Ea/2.303·R) · (T2 − T1)/(T1·T2).

Activation energy is the minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess. Threshold energy = average energy + Ea.

sections.heading: Collision Theory
sections.body:
According to collision theory (for bimolecular gas reactions):

Rate = ZAB · e−Ea/RT, where ZAB is the collision frequency.

Not every collision leads to reaction. Two requirements:

  1. Energy barrier: collisions must have energy ≥ Ea (fraction = e−Ea/RT).
  2. Orientation: molecules must collide in the right geometry. Captured by the steric factor (P) in the modified equation: Rate = P·ZAB·e−Ea/RT.

Hence Arrhenius's pre-exponential factor A = P·ZAB.

Effective collision = collision with sufficient energy AND proper orientation.

sections.heading: Effect of a Catalyst
sections.body:
A catalyst alters the rate of a reaction without being consumed. It provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy, so a larger fraction of molecules now have energy ≥ Ea'.

A catalyst:

  • Lowers Ea for both forward and reverse reactions equally;
  • Does not change ΔG, ΔH, ΔU, or K of the reaction;
  • Does not change the equilibrium composition, only how fast equilibrium is reached;
  • Catalyses both forward and reverse reactions to the same extent.

Examples: Pt for catalytic hydrogenation; V2O5 in contact process (SO2 → SO3); Fe in Haber process (N2 + H2 → NH3).

summary: Chemical kinetics studies how fast reactions proceed and the factors controlling rate. Rate law (rate = k[A]^x[B]^y) is empirical; order can differ from stoichiometry, while molecularity must equal an integer for an elementary step. Integrated forms give zero-order linear decays ([R] = [R]_0 − kt) and first-order exponential decays (k = (2.303/t) log [R]_0/[R], t_1/2 = 0.693/k). The Arrhenius equation k = A·e^(−Ea/RT) captures temperature dependence; plotting log k vs 1/T extracts E_a. Collision theory adds a steric factor P. A catalyst speeds both forward and reverse reactions equally by lowering E_a and never shifts equilibrium.
pitfalls: Confusing order (experimental, can be fractional/zero) with molecularity (count of molecules in elementary step, integer).
Quoting wrong units of k for the order — practice deriving them from rate = (M/s) = k·M^n.
Forgetting the 2.303 conversion when switching between ln and log forms.
Assuming half-life is constant for all orders — only first-order has t_{1/2} independent of [R]_0.
Treating a catalyst as shifting equilibrium — it does not.
Ignoring the orientation factor; a high-collision system may still react slowly if P is small.
